sections.title: Overview
sections.content: From around 20 weeks gestation onward, the gravid uterus can compress the inferior vena cava and aorta when the mother is supine. This may worsen hypotension, reduce venous return, and compromise both maternal and foetal perfusion, so left uterine displacement should be considered whenever a late-pregnancy patient must remain supine.

sections.title: Techniques
sections.content:
**Conscious patient:**
- Position in the left lateral position if tolerated and clinically appropriate

**When the patient must remain supine:**
1. Use manual left uterine displacement when possible, especially during resuscitation or major trauma care
2. If manual displacement is not practical, tilt the patient or place a wedge under the right side to shift the uterus leftward
3. Reassess blood pressure, symptoms, and overall perfusion after repositioning

**During CPR:**
- Manual left uterine displacement is preferred because it preserves a firm surface for compressions

sections.title: Key Points
sections.content:
- Use this whenever late pregnancy and supine positioning are likely to coexist, not only when symptoms are already obvious
- Any safe positioning that relieves uterine compression is acceptable
- During arrest, CPR quality and immediate uterine displacement take priority over complex tilt setups
- Document the gestation if known and what manoeuvre was used
